author: @ToninFightsEntropy
text:
Gonna do some thinking out loud before I watch this video: I need to think about this sort of thing more.. for my gravity wall, my best start plan so far is a pendulum double-driving a gear which doesn't backdrive, which could mechanically regulate the release of a gearbox pushed by a heavy weight.. for a constant output, and now I realise oh wait I need to NOT have a constant output 🤣goddamn, starting over 
So I need power demand to make the gearbox go.. So that generates on demand from the gravity + tension springs. Explosion risk is only shrapnel..
But for moderating how much the gearbox turns, slowing the gears down means throwing energy away unless I do it with a second generator that doesn't go to the load (an input generator for lifting.. which to operate at the same time means two sets of weights or for the release to be bypassed/replaced by all my other energy harvesting inputs until demand isn't met.. or maybe some modular system of little generators that are all turned when it goes but slide along a rail to couple with different load outputs, moved automatically by something derived from a resistance measurement...? Maybe something that triggers at a certain voltage or temperature could activate heating of nitinol springs to shove them along.. though maybe I could do this easier with gears and a spring, but then bits have to lock.. how to not waste power in this is a challenge. I do want to do this all as plainly mechanically as possible.
OK watching the video now while my confused ideas float 😅

author: @russellzauner
text:
For people asking more details of the setup and circuits used:
Every component has a data sheet (the Arduino has schematics and board plots available, being an assembly) and every data sheet has not only test circuits used to characterize the component's specifications (where the charts and diagrams come from) but if the component manufacturer is worth their salt they'll also offer application notes of many typical use cases so people can *learn* the design rules behind circuits (so they can actually *sell* components, especially new/novel ones).  Once you look at those, you'll know what questions to really ask - like how to handle thermal trips, overcurrent protection, et al. because things like component value selection and why that particular circuit will already be answered.
I learned a lot of things that I couldn't understand on paper because I had directions on how to build a test circuit - lol I'd have no idea how to use an energy harvesting IC or why it was able to do what it does if not for app notes/test circuits.  Bonus is I know what circuit values to change to make it work for different input sources and produce different output voltages/currents because I saw it in the datasheet first and *then* found a breakout board online that very conveniently was exactly the test circuit already built for me.  I still can't build a buck/boost regulator from scratch but I can swap components and measure outputs well enough.

author: @koaasst
text: ahh im building one now with a LM317 but havnt found proper resistors yet laying around

replies.author: @jeremiahbullfrog9288
replies.text: LM317 is a linear device, which works differently than the circuits he presented. All of the "extra" energy is dissipated as heat with the linear circuits. Imagine an electronically controlled rheostat.

replies.author: @koaasst
replies.text: @jeremiahbullfrog9288 my goal was to build one from scratch to go from 12v to 5v using parts laying around the scrap piles and chatgpt led me to the transistor route. i found the lm317 instead of the LM3506 or whichever it is that is built in resistors so now i have to find a 240 and a 720 ohm combo or something like that, and a couple caps across the leads. then i can run a 12v wire a good distance and drop to 5v right at the camera

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text: @jeremiahbullfrog9288 now you have to explain rheostat lol and then you'll have to explain wire wound resistors and THEN you'll probably have to explain inductance, resistance, impedance, and THEN you'll have to explain phase relationships....I remember the time someone asked me what a triac was and I said "a diode with a switch" *deer in headlights look*  ;-)

replies.author: @jeremiahbullfrog9288
replies.text: @russellzauner Good point but also... i don't have to explain anything. I left a breadcrumb for OP to do their own research if they desire. I'm okay if my input isn't helpful to them at this time. (Side note: gender-neutralizing singular pronouns by substituting the plural "they" is excruciating lol)

replies.author: @jyvben1520
replies.text:
voltage divider is a couple of resistors, premade computer => premade microprocessor, 
a transistor replaces the switching part of the mechanical example (electrical relay)

author: @10sheds21
text: God bless Amazon, and thank you I almost kept up but wouldn't trust myself.

replies.author: @jeremiahbullfrog9288
replies.text: Trust is earned. You have to get your feet wet to build that confidence. Start small and work your way up -- and stay away from Mains Voltage (the outlets in your wall) until you get enough experience!
